Intro1: Causal reasoning is the ability to understand how and why things happen. It helps us tell real causes from coincidences and evaluate what might occur if we intervene or make a change (Pearl, 2009). These skills are important in everyday life. For example, they can help us interpret health advice, assess policy claims, and judge whether a product, habit, or decision is actually responsible for an observed result. Causal thinking is a difficult but incredibly valuable skill.
Intro2: In the age of AI, causal reasoning faces a serious risk. AI systems are brilliant at finding patterns and associations, but they cannot determine whether one thing causes another. When people rely on AI-generated summaries or predictions without questioning the underlying logic, they could become vulnerable to false or misleading arguments. Maintaining causal reasoning skills helps us question conclusions, ask the right follow-ups, and (most importantly) helps us avoid accepting polished explanations built on shaky foundations.
Practice1: Causal reasoning can improve with deliberate practice that focuses on thinking. Exercises that ask us to identify confounders, consider alternative explanations, separate causes from mechanisms, or reason about counterfactuals build the mental habits needed to evaluate causal claims (Sloman & Lagnado, 2015). Practicing these scenarios should help you recognize common pitfalls and become more skilled at spotting weak arguments. The goal here is to help build your ability to think carefully about “what causes what” in a sea of complex (and often misleading) information.
Ref_Pearl: Pearl, J. (2009). Causality: Models, reasoning, and inference (2nd ed.). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.
Ref_Sloman: Sloman, S. A., & Lagnado, D. A. (2015). Causal inference in thinking and decision making. Annual Review of Psychology, 66, 245–267.
